response.description_long:
Warehouses rarely buy all their automation from one vendor. Each robot brand arrives with its own fleet manager, map, and queue, and they don't talk to each other. Orders stall while one fleet idles and another backs up. Palletmind addresses this problem with vendor-neutral orchestration software that connects upward to warehouse management systems (WMS) like Manhattan and Blue Yonder, and downward to each vendor's fleet manager through its API.

Every few seconds, a task-allocation solver decides which robot should do which job, balancing travel time, battery state, congestion, and order priority across the entire floor. A shared traffic map prevents deadlocks at intersections and dock doors. Before go-live, operators can use a digital twin to simulate their busiest day against a proposed layout and see throughput without moving a single rack.

Palletmind is used by third-party logistics providers (3PLs) and retailers operating mixed fleets of AMRs, conveyor systems, and pick stations across North America and Europe. The company reports that sites running its software see 20 to 35 percent more picks per hour from the same fleet, and integration timelines drop to 8 weeks instead of the 6 months a custom middleware build typically takes.

Palletmind generates revenue through an annual license per site, sized by robot count. Founded in Atlanta in 2020 by engineers from an AMR manufacturer, the company now has 61 people across Atlanta and Rotterdam.
